speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Eugene Settle took place at his home in Moscow on January 13th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I showed a picture of a sign that was made of tin and it had ducked up on a fence out between our place and school. And there was a train and a fellow running to catch the train in a suitcase and said, going to the Boston. But I never did know just where the Boston store was. And then I heard that I had been reading some memoirs of Earl David, the one he sent for the founders of the David store here. And I heard that there was a memoirs in there. And in that he said, he spoke about this Williamson taking over the Boston store. So in other words, what was Williamson when I was a kid, when I got to know him, that was old Boston. Afternoon and boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you, when you came to town with your father, what did he do in town besides get groceries? Did he go and visit and spend time talking to people?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, no. Just like any other farmer, he'd buy groceries and then of course he'd always visit on the corner and speak to somebody. But my father was a guy that would hang around the pool halls or pastimes like that for the only time. When I was a little kid, when I used to come to town with my dad, we used to come in, of course, in David's store now. That side door on 3rd street, that's why they had a grocery store there. And my folks did all their trading there. We'd always go in there and they always had one of these big potbelly stoves in there, you know. Had a good fire in there. Any time we'd go in there and get one, get the groceries and then get about everything you wanted. I guess we wanted to go anywhere else. But David was the main place that we went. He went.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And in the later years, when I was playing in the years, he always... Ralston's store there, Tomosco Grocer's store, that's why he always played with Ralston's store. But that's funny, why they'd just come down to buy groceries. Of course, you always find somebody to visit with.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But he never was no hands, that's it, hanging around the pool halls and things like that. He never did that. So that's what a lot of them are. They come around and come down and they go to the pool hall and get warm. A lot of them like to play cards. Some of them play cards. Some of them... such as that, pool or something like that. When I was a kid, there was about one, two... there were about four pool halls in Moscow then, the card tables. Where the farmers used to gather when they come to town in the wintertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the past time? Huh? Did you know the past time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You used to go in there when you were a young man?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I used to go in there when I was a young man. That's why I hang out. Well, even after I fell in life, we were first married. I come to town in the wintertime, always before we'd go to the past time. We always had a good fire there and we always had a lunch counter there, so you could go there and get your lunch and get warm. Then I tell you another thing about the past time, if there's anybody that you wanted to see, whenever you go to the past time and hang out and have, pretty soon you'd come in. It was sure quite a gathering place for the farmers. It's a place that never closed. And they always had a good fire there. You always get good warm, get warm there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And everybody, if you come to town, the farmer would come to town, why, as soon as you got his team taken care of, you'd take out for the past time, right?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course, then he'd get warm, get him a hot cup of coffee, and he'd always see all his friends would be there. If you're looking for anybody, well, go to the past time, get your own beer in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how come, what made that so much the most popular place?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I tell you, it was a good place to warm. As I said before, there's too many cars, you know, well, after cars, so just come in there and drive, drive like we would out there, out ten or twelve miles to town, or ten miles to town, with a sled and a team. Well, first thing you want to know, you don't want to go to space and get warmed up.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And as I say, they had a big lunch counter there, and they had lots of pool tables, and I think they had about five pool tables, and I don't know how many car tables they had, and some of those farmers, they liked to play rummy, and what else did they play?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They played a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they play poker?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, they didn't play no poker, they had no poker. No, I wasn't in town.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't know if they ever did. They might have in early days, they played poker, had poker games in the saloons, I don't know, that was before my time, they were, I don't remember nothing about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But past time, that was during Prohibition, right?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's when, oh, no. Yeah, they had a confectionary counter there, and lunch counter, and as I say, car tables, pool tables, and so on and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there other places in Moscow to go if a guy wanted to?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah, there's others, there's, see there's, there was a shorty of corner, used to run a pool, same place, the same kind, and then, and Robins had one, there's all smaller places, and then there's, in at the corner of the Moscow Hotel, where their, where their,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: warrior, no, that was, that was, no, I think that was just billiards, that was just billiards, oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you shoot pool?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I saw them, but not too much. I had my brother next to me, three years younger than I am, he was quite a pool player, but I played some that I never played much, much pool.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I played a little bit, but not much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were a young man, you know, what would you do for entertainment?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, when I was a kid in the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Old enough, you know, old enough to go out and do what you wanted?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Uh huh, well, I was...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Until after we moved over here to town, and of course we lived, I started high school when I got here, I was at high school, but when we went over in the country there, well, then we had to, we had to play baseball, and after we moved to town here, of course,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: we didn't do too much, as I say, we took in the wintertime, we took in a lot of, but big time was taking in basketball games at high school and university and such as that, and going back and forth over to pool, and for all the school sports in the wintertime, that's what our biggest thing was in the wintertime, and of course, pastime was in the wintertime, and we did quite a bit of hunting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to movies? Huh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Did you go to movies in the... Yeah, we went to movies, yeah, that's right, we went to movies too quite a bit, whenever we went, weekend we always went to movies, probably about once a week, sometimes more than that, when it's done with the skids, which is when we used to, plus the Saturday nights we'd go downtown to go to movies, sometimes we used to do that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And we went to another thing in the wintertime, we had quite a stockout on the ranch and sleigh, and we used to take sleighing parties out there, that was a big deal for us on the weekends, take it like when I was in high school, we used to take university kids over to Pullman and back and forth, and that way, or a different round, or just go off for a couple, three hours drive in a sleigh and party, you know, you had to possibly do that. What would you do with that, you just took up a cutter to the... Oh, we had a bob sled, not the big sled, and probably put a hay rack on it, you know, and put some straw in it, and these university kids, they'd get their first their own blankets, get it in there,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: probably five or six couples, and we'd take them, they usually know where they wanted to go, and we'd take them home. That's why we made quite a bit of spending money, that way we couldn't win a bunch. That seemed like we always had something to do, and if we didn't, why we... weekends, why if we didn't have nothing to do weekends, why then we used, as I say, we used to get a lot of hunting. I just had a letter from a friend, a kid who kind of grew up with us, who we'd known a long time, he lived up to Kellogg, and he wrote me a letter the other day, and he got to talking about the fun we used to have on weekends, and he said, you know, we're going hunting over on this Paradise Hill over here, and up in the Moscow mongrel.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he said, but my brother, he was climbing up a bank, and my brother had a shotgun, and he got to the end of the barrel full of snow, and he never noticed it, and I don't think, I don't know what he shot at, I think it was a snowshoe rabbit, and it just flowed to the end of the barrel, just bailed it out, just about two inches, you know, just swelled the end of the barrel out there about two, looked like a bell. He said, that's snow on the end of the barrel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you guys go out and camp out, or just go out for the day?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, just for the day, just for the day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you usually get some?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh no, we just did a lot of hiking, there's more, we never got much. Sometime we'd get a, in the wintertime we'd probably kill a snowshoe rabbit or two, and sometimes we'd get a shot at a coyote, but we didn't have just seasonable, but it, but we liked, we enjoyed hiking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you went afoot and not with horses? Huh? You went afoot?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, oh yeah, oh yes, we went foot.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And when us teenagers, we were out there in the country, we only had saddle horses, and lots of, lots of Saturdays, we'd get on the, four or five of us, get a saddle, and we'd ride into town, just ride around town, and probably didn't have money enough to go to the show with us, so I had to ride around us, and back, just, fast as time went.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You know, I was thinking that we talked a fair bit about other things, but we hadn't talked at all about the farming that you did, your own farming career, which, from what you did mention to me, wasn't all that long, and you didn't think it was that great, but you did farm for some time. Farm, oh yes, we farm, we farm till, till I was married in 24, we farmed up until, I guess it was about 30, 37 I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now along? Well what did you have to start with when you farmed?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I rented 200, had 240 acres of land rented, and I had 8 to 10 head horses, and farming all fit, same thing about that. That was for, for a quit, for a quit, why I, I cleaned my horse off and got a, got a small, small tractor, and I used a tractor the last few years I farmed, I used to have a little tractor, small one, 15, oh.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I think farming, I think farming was a pretty good life myself, I liked, I liked, I liked the farm, I liked the stock, I liked the full stock of it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But we farm, we farm, my wife and I, of course we got married in the Depression,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and we had a pretty, we had a pretty tough, pretty tough fed, and we, it was cheap, and that's when we quit the farm, and then we quit, and then after we quit the farm, then World War II broke out, and things went up, we just missed it, we should have stayed with it. We talked about it quite a bit, that's the Del Wafer we started staying with, and we wish we had stayed with the farm a little longer, but she think we turned out pretty good, anyway, if we were, she's good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you, when you say you started in the Depression, you mean the one after, you mean after World War I and the farm crisis went down?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We started, we started farming in 24,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and that's when we, that was during the, right after World War I Depression, and then we quit farming, we quit just, we quit just before World War II broke out.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, you, you were saying, you said to me once before that, that the equipment that was coming in was making it harder for, and harder for the small farmers with forests. Oh yes it was, yeah that's why, you know, the last part of, the last part of my farm, you know the tractors was coming in, and of course, a lot of the farms that they bought were pretty good size farms, they'd buy tractors, you know, and then it was making it pretty hard for a farmer to farm with horses to lease land then, you know, everybody wanted to, wanted to lease it, so a fellow had a tractor because he could get the work in and get it quicker than he could with horses. I really don't know how we got a kid to get our work did with horses though, when we got a horse, they got it in, they'd copse, save the cops too, but guys they'd do it so much faster with a tractor.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So horses, well that was, well when I quit farming, that was the beginning of the end of the little farmer. When I started farming there was lots of farmers, farming 160 and 80, not 160 and 80 acre farms, but you don't see those anymore.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, some, let's not try to host a town or something like that, but there were lots of farmers that, lots of 80 acre farms and 160 acre farms, and then, well, we started farming but now, but after the tractors, well then the big farmer, the big farmer got bigger, and they just pushed the little farmer clear off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see that happen to yourself?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I see it happen to myself, yeah, just about time that, just before we quit farming, it was the same way now, just the same with me now. Now we, I farmed 200 and 240 acre farm that for quite a while, and then I lost that to a fellow that had, that had a tractor, it was the same fellow I ran around with, he was under the parlor, and I lost that place to him, and then we bought 160, and the fellow at the bank that I had this, that I bought this 160 acre farm, they had another 160 acres about two miles from where I live, what I bought, that didn't have no building on it, and he was, he was gonna let, I was gonna get, he was gonna let me have that, but the fellow that was farming was in debt to him pretty bad, and he said as soon as he got out of debt to him, I could have that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But it's all around me there, it's all, it wasn't the one, oh, we got these old big farmers, those that got tractors, well they just took the farm and had,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: it was pretty hard for a fellow that was farming horses to get in, to lease any land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, with 160 acres of land, that's what you were farming then, when you had, when you had the place, was it hard to make a living with that, or horses?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, yes, well I'll tell you, if a fellow had,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: a fellow, if he had his, if he had his land here, and he had, he was farming 160 acres of horses, and had him a few cows, and diversified, and a few hogs, he can make a living on it, where he didn't have to depend on wheat and oats and grain alone, you know, where he had a few milk cows and, you know, he had hogs on the side, a few beef, something like that, a fellow could make a pretty good living, a small farmer, but just that's, as they say, that's hard to work with, why you just, straight grain farm, farm was for the grain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what you did, did you have cattle and hogs too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we had a few cattle, and a few hogs, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember very well what happened when the expression hit here, out here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I remember pretty well, yep.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I remember expression pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you say the kind of effect was that it had on people around here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, you take a,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: there was no money, the banks were all closed, there was no money available, and of course they bombed the poor farmer, the poor guy worked on WPA, as you might have heard of it, I suppose at the time of that, worked on WPA for a while, I never did though, I never did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then we took a flour, took wheat down to Lewiston, down to Asotin, there was a flour mill down there, we took wheat down there, and got our own flour, got our own wheat made into flour, that's where we didn't get by, and then they had substitutes, we couldn't have so much flour anyway, and before that they had substitutes, they had barley flour, you couldn't buy this and you couldn't buy that, and you hadn't dried gasoline, it was the one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was in the Depression, you couldn't buy, you only get so much flour ground?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You couldn't, you weren't supposed to have, you only get so much flour around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Around?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, you couldn't have it, just over the course, some farmers had a lot of it, had got it and buried it up in the hay barn in H, so they wouldn't catch them with it, that way it was more than they were supposed to have, some were supposed to have substitutes, you see, barley flour, I don't know what all it was, but I know the substitutes.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But of course you take the one we were farming, and then they just sell the land, because they didn't take much flour for us, that's how far it lasted us, you'd take each farmer, they had big families, they had huge lots of farmers, they couldn't get enough white flour to take care of them, they had to buy huge substitutes and one thing or another.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You see the flour, I forget what else, I don't remember what else it was we couldn't get,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you find that you could make it through okay? Did you find you had to go in debt then, during the Depression?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's the worst thing for me, I was in debt when I got married, I bought my outfit to farm with, I didn't have money enough to pay for it, and I went in debt for that part of it, I had to buy, take quite a little bit of machinery to farm, 240 acres of ground, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you have?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: 240 acres. No, I mean what machinery did you... Oh, I had to have a plow, and about 8 can't hit horses, and a binder, and a drill, and different spring tooth and a disc,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course one thing about me, there's another fellow that started up just, my neighbor was older than I was, but he just grew up on the farm and he got married again, and he rented some, he had a place right next to me there, so he didn't, he like myself, he didn't have much money and much machinery, so we traded, exchanged back and forth that way quite a little bit, up to us all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did he have some, what did he have that you didn't have in machinery?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: see we bought a disc together, I know we bought a disc, no it wasn't a disc of spring tooth hair, we bought one of those together, we bought one of the best cleaners, we went together and bought one of those, and then I think the disc of our...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In the wagon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So were you still in debt when the Depression hit? Were you still owe money?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's what I was in, that's what I say, I was in debt when I, I was farming during the Depression and so that's why I was in debt.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well I got my outfit, my outfit didn't cost me too much, I bought a lot of my horse's livestock, because I was working for a big farmer out here, south and west of town, and he had bought him a big tractor, he'd bought a tractor, and so he sold me what horses I wanted, I just picked them out of his herd, and he sold them to my rare sheep.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Horses and harness and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you'd been working for this guy before you got married?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah I worked for him through harvest time last time, I sold sacks all his combine, before I was married.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I got a pretty good break in buying my livestock,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and I was in debt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see other people go under during that time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, yeah there's a lot of them. Lots of sales, they were farmed, they were full, people just couldn't pay up, they just fell out and tried something else.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I saw a lot of them go under, and some of them just quit, couldn't make anything, so they just quit, and then as I say, the tractors were coming in, and the fellow that was able to buy him a tractor, just like this is what I worked for, you know, I'd shoot those fellows. If you were renting the land, if there was somebody he wanted, why he just about did it, they wouldn't let you shoot.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, it's still the same way today now. The little, as I say before, there's no such thing as a little farmer anymore.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Unless there's somebody that owns the 240 acres of land, that's probably still living on it, and his boys are farmed for him, or he's got his land leased out to somebody else, but he's living in the property, but they were on the buildings, but if somebody else was farming the place, it's probably farming the lettuce I was making.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the people in town, in Moscow, had a rougher time during the Depression than the people out in the country did?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, I don't think they had any rougher time than anybody, than anywhere else, but I imagine that there's lots of town people, poor people, they had a hard time getting on these government sponsored jobs, on WPA and such and such, a lot of farmers the same way. But that's one thing about us on the farm,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: we raised so much of our eats, we had our own meat and potatoes, such as that, we raised that, we had that, we raised that, while I was a poor fellow in town, he had to buy all of his, so he had to get some money from, have some money somewhere to get, to live on. While we could, we could raise our own meat and our own potatoes, while we had to basically, while living right there. And then of course we can, can a lot of fruit in the summertime, you know, when you can get it. We had a cellar full of fruit, canned goods,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: of course in those days we didn't can, we didn't can as many vegetables like we do nowadays, now we can all the vegetables, we didn't can so many vegetables. But we fed, the farmer, he fed, the living was concerned, he fared a little better than the poor, I mean the poor farmer, he fared a little bit better than the poor guy in my town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Roosevelt, Franklin Roosevelt when he got in?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I have to say one thing for Franklin Roosevelt, he kind of, see in that worst depression, I don't know if you remember, but it was under Hoover, and Franklin Roosevelt come in, he started all these WPA's and all that stuff, you know, and give the poor man a chance to get out and do a little work to make a little something. He kind of started a small role or something, I think, to give a lot of credit for that, because he made jobs for a lot of these fellows around town, these WPA's, and I don't know how many different of those jobs that he did have in all forms, shapes and sizes. So he got, he fixed it so the poor man could make a little money.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Did you and Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Settle date for a very long time before you moved?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, about four years after we first met I guess, I guess we were 20 and we didn't get married until 24. Of course we didn't, we wasn't like lots of people, that's why we lived in the same town like she lived. She lived up here towards Blue, so the Department of Country and I lived down here, and we had a car, we probably didn't see each other, it was probably twice, I guess it was twice a month, something like that, I got up at the weekend there up there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We went together every week like most people are when they're speaking, with us, three of two four apart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you get married? Where did you get married?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Walla Walla. After we got engaged, by then she went to California, she had two sisters down in California, and the one sister, she thought she'd go to California and get her job and work that summer, and she thought she'd have something to help buy furniture with. So that's why she went to, she stayed the first year, and then that year I batched and lived on the farm, and then she came up from, come home, and she came home that fall around before Christmas time, and then I met her at Walla Walla and we got married in Walla Walla, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she work at? Did she leave out in California?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She just did domestic work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Aren't you down there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you offered a job at the grain growers, or were you offered a job? What made you, how did you wind up quitting the farming?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'll tell you, I wound up, I wasn't getting anywhere. I would farm and I'd tell what little on that 160, and then I was paying for a place, and then I had other debts on the side, and I worked beside my cloth, and I worked out all I could, and by golly, I just wasn't, I wasn't getting anywhere. And it wasn't what I owed on the place that was bothering me, it was the outside debts that was where they were calling me, because the people that I bought the place from, the bank in Lewiston,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: they sure wanted me to stay with them. There were all kinds of chances, and as I said a while ago, I should have took them, I shouldn't have, I think lots of times I should have stayed with the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are the outside debts you're thinking of?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I, oh, I had, sometimes I'd buy, have, buy, buy feed, and something, I'd borrow money at the bank, and of course I always got that feedback, I'd have it, but then,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and then I, well our biggest debt though was, was, seemed like a pain for machinery,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and of course we had, we had a few cows, and you know, several claims that we got most of our groceries, we didn't, we got our groceries most of what we owed to that way, but, but most of my debt was, was, was mostly for machinery.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And it seemed like every fall, every spring of the year I always had to buy feed from my stock to, I'd run out of hay during the winter time, and I didn't have enough feed to carry me through, so next I'd have to use that to buy grain, buy, buy some grain in the winter time, spring for getting my crop in and such as that, and I'd use that over that, and of course I'd have it all, had it all on time, of course I didn't have no money, and so, till I got my crop in, and at times, it's time you're trying to make your place payment and your taxes, and then you work out, you didn't have money enough to go around, so that was my trouble. I didn't have no back tonight, for a fact.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When I wasn't alone, there's a lot of us the same way, but I worked hard, worked the way awful hard. That's why the fella that I bought the place from, that's why he didn't want to see me leave the place, he, they want me to stay with it. And they made me a pretty good proposition too, they told me that if I would just give them a mortgage on everything that I had,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and then they would take care of me then, see if I hadn't got the machinery they wanted, and of course I'd just be working for them, you see, practically. But the way that, as I say, if the war broke out and wheat went up, grain went up, well then I would have, that would have been in my favor, I would have, it probably took me a year or two to pay out, but I think I would have come out in the long one, grain went up and then the price of land, I said, oh the price of land started climbing, so I would have come out in the long one, but at that time I would have, it was 80 and 160 joining me there that I think I would have, I would have bought to go with what I had and decided to give me a pretty good 400 acre farm there, you see, and I would have been in pretty good shape. No slubbing back, no that kind of thing, I think I would have made it in a good shape. They would have let you do that by the extra man. They would have let you buy the extra man. I can't believe they would have, I can't believe they would have, I think they would have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you decided at the time to turn them down, huh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I, so I, so I got, so I thought I just got so tired of it, of being in debt, that I tried to do something else and I, then I bought a truck and I did some trucking on all wood and I started all the way from Brickyard down here and I worked on that for a year or two. And then I, then when the war broke out, well then I went, I went, took the welding and I went to, I went down to Portland to stay the year and worked on the fence. I worked on it a year and that's when I worked hard down there and I made good money and every time I get a check, I send it home to my wife here, so she, we complete our debts and bought this place. And then I, I come home and a fellow, a big farmer over here, a fellow that I farm with, where I rented this, he spotted this part of the land from me when we first started farming. He was, he was farming a lot of the old land then and, and he wanted somebody to run his combine and he wanted me to, I was in Portland and so I, he was talking about going on a strike down there anyway so I left the fence and come up here to go to work for him and then I, for him that fall.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then from there I, then this fellow that, the manager of Lake Oak County Grain Growers then, I knew him when I was farming down there, you see. He'd school up and then he,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and somebody, a friend of mine started to work for him before he'd go and told him that I was coming back from, he told me I was going to be coming back, told him that I was coming back from Portland pretty soon and so he told, he told a friend to tell him, he says, when I, he says, as soon as he gets back, why don't you tell him to come and see me?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So this fellow come, told me about it, so I went to see him and in the meantime this fellow that wanted me to run his combine, he had, he had got, he had found somebody else to run his combine and so he had to start before I got in, before I got home. So I went to see this man by the name of Simpson and he wanted me to go to work as Joel, out here Joel, warehouse for him. So I went to work with him and I worked out that Joel for,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: for one year, for one first year I worked out that under a fellow by the name of Joe Ann. He was, he was running the warehouse out there and he, he quit and went to farm and stuff and pricing went up so he, he had a farm and he just quit, he makes, he makes it on the farm so he quit and then I, then, the next, then the next year this Simpson, he gave me the, he, he put me in charge out there, running, running Joel, see, running the warehouse out there as Joel. So I run the first year, that year, I run, run the warehouse under him, running Joel, I was a foreman under Joel and this is, and Simpson, that fall, first of all, he, then he retired and the fellow was bookkeeping. They want him to take, they want him to take the manager of the whole outfit of the company and he said, I'll take it under one's consideration. If you let me have a warehouse foreman. They have a man to look after all the warehouses, they had a but, Joel and Powell and Troy and Derry and, and Kennedy Ford over here and Esther Station. And my fellow take care of all the warehouses, take care of the labor and everything. Well, they, when I told him they wanted to know who I had, who, who did, did he have a man in mind for the job and he said, yes I do. And he said, he wanted to be in the shuttle, so they let him have it. So I got the, so I got to be a big shot in the company, in about three years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you, do you know this guy who had been the bookkeeper? Did you know him?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I knew him, I just got a claim to him since I started working for the company. I just known him, of course I didn't know him before I started working for the company, but I knew the man who hired me, Simpson, I knew him. As I said, I had business with him before I was on the farm. I knew him, but I didn't know these books, I didn't know his bookkeeper, until I started working for the company. Well you told me when we were talking before that, that when you worked under him as the warehouse superintendent, that you had real good, good support from him. Yes I did, I did, I had good support from him, he better. Yes sir, he showed, he, he, he showed good back of me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes sir.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He depended on me because I knew, I knew Grain,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and I knew warehouse, I knew that, I knew that end of the business. Of course he, he knew the book part of it, but he wasn't too good at that time, he wasn't too much as an outside part of it, and I knew it. So it was just a very good thing, and so he, he really backed me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was a fine guy.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was going to ask you why, why you, you took out a long, what made you get along so well, but I guess you're, you're telling me the answer, by saying that you, that he depended on you. Yes he did, he depended on me a whole lot, he bet he did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: As I said, of course I knew the outside of the, I knew the, the elevator part of the business, too much better than he did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So we worked together, we just made a plan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the main responsibilities that you had, and you were doing it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well I, I did all the, I hired all the warehouse help, and I had to see that, and then I had to, I, you see I had Joel and Troy and Kennedy Ford, and those places, I had to, I used to pick up the tickets out there, and it, it, it, especially at Derry, I, I used to build out, most of us would go up there and build out the cars, sell cars, I'd take, I'd go up and build out the cars, and of course I'd build them out here too, and they'd build out most of them, and I took care of all the building of cars that was out these neighborhood stations.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I did all the hiring and firing and, and kept brooms and bought all the sacks and brooms and wine that we used.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I bought some machinery for the cleaning plant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a bit of a cold? What? You still have a bit of a cold?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I haven't dealt with my cold entirely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, did you have a pretty, any, being and working in the grain growers, did you have a, any better sense than, than the farmer in the field about, about how prices were going? I mean, did you have a pretty good idea of the direction?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I, well, you know, in a way I knew it before they'd do it because I, I get it at the office, you see, I, I was right close to the main office of the, in the manager, so I usually knew what they knew. So I, I really knew it before most of the farmers did, of course a lot of them at the same time I did, a lot of wheat and something, you know. I didn't know much about first prices was concerned, why you just, you could just kind of farm your own idea about whether wheat is going to go up or down, and of course that's concerned. But if they got any dope, by the end of course I used, I used to got it right off the bat. They asked me, of course I know a lot of them, when,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: the last part I had my office down that big elevator on Main Street, that big one right down the last one, the one out of town, that's why. And lots of guys would come in there and talk to me instead of going to the main office, and of course I knew, usually knew what they knew and discussed, and then lots of times I didn't know why I'd get on the phone and find out for them, so I had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You give me a little idea of how, I've never played that, that the market so to speak to figure out when to sell and when to hold on to it, but how did that work anyway? I mean, how did, what were the farmers thinking about when they tried to calculate whether the market was going to go up or down?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'll tell you this,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: it's pretty hard to tell whether they go up or down or not, it just depends on if they get a headline that there's a big wheat crop in Australia or Russia or somewhere, whether there's a drought or something, they could kind of figure on what's the place, whether they figure on, they kind of guess on that, well that'll help the wheat farmers. Now when I was a kid, the farmers used to say that Russia controlled the wheat market of the United States. If they had a big crop in Russia, iron prices were down. If they had a short crop in Russia, then the iron prices were a little better. Now I heard that when I was a little kid, that Russia controlled the wheat market of the United States, but I found out after I got older, I found out that later, but that wasn't all together, that's the world market.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I can't see hardly how that in those days the wheat was as cheap as it was, grain, figuring that when everybody on the farm had, he had from six to twenty head horse and mules and cattle to feed. You just think of the grain that takes the feed, although they don't have those anymore now. The big farmers, the biggest farmers you got, a lot of them don't have a horse on the place or a cow or a chicken.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So that dumped a lot, that dumped an awful lot of grain on the, more grain on the market.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, as a general rule, do you have a feeling about whether it was better for a farmer to sell after he got his crop in or whether he really did better by hanging on to the winter to see what would happen?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'll tell you, Sam,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: oh I know, I got my ideas through just working around with different farmers when I was a kid, you know, and I worked for one farmer,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and he, down here at Genesee or ..., you probably run into some of them or heard of them down, some of these kids or grandkids down there yet. And he was farming quite a bit of good land down there, and he got a little bit of it, and I worked for him different years, four or five, and that man, when he got his last sack in the warehouse, he sold it. And he died a wealthy man. And I worked for one farmer, and he was out of town, he was the 12th, 12th little guy ever off, he always wanted, he always hold his wheat for a higher price, when he could get two dollars he wanted three.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, he wound up the bank had it, got his place. But I think that, I think that, of course I never had no big lot of wheat to sell, and I never did. And I used this old mine right after I got in the warehouse, because I had bills pushing me that I wanted to get taken care of, of course I had the thrashing bill and such that to give, so I used this old right away, but if I was, but to gamble on wheat, I always figured out I might sell part of it, half of it or something like that, then hold some for a while along the first year to see how, what the price is going to be. But I think that year in and year out, that Genesee farmer had the best idea of them all. When you get to the warehouse, sell it. Then it's off your mind. Then it's off your mind, I bet you. Because when you hold it, the price goes up a little, you're paying storage on that wheat too, you see.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And so, I think the man that, when he puts it, when he gets in the warehouse, when he sells it, I think he did better, and then this, another fellow that did that, and this, Bob Peterson, this attorney down in Moscow, you probably heard of him, but his dad, he's a big farm, he owned all, he owned all, owned all this, this new part of Is that Klaus Peterson? Huh? Is that Klaus Peterson?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, Klaus Peterson. He farmed down towards Washington, down in the lower former road down there, and he owned quite a bit of land, had a big pretty good size ranch down there, and passed up here in town and had a ranch over in that country where I live, farm. And he, he followed that, that's what he followed. And old Klaus Peterson, he was the rich man. He left a big estate.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So, just gambling on wheat, and then, then I, then some farmers, I heard of them selling wheat and buying on, buying on a border trade in the margin. They got gambling on that way, but, but I think the guy that made the money is, when he got his wheat in the warehouse, is, he didn't gamble on it, he sold it. He sold it to the farmers and put his, put his money in the bank or invested in something else, and we didn't. It wasn't like I was that owed it all for this, so it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you run into farmers that didn't trust the co -op? Huh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Did you run into farmers that didn't trust the co -op and thought that they were getting cheated on their, on their wheat, they weren't getting what they should get, and they weren't getting a good enough grade, and they weren't getting Well, I, I ran, I ran into farmers that didn't, that didn't say too much about, seemed like it wasn't too much on the wheat, but they was, seemed like their big argument more than anything was on the peas. They didn't, they, they were satisfied on the, on the, on the pea grades. I know that I, your sample, you take it to warehouse and you turn it in, and then, then they'd come back and want to get one another sample. Some of them want three or four different samples on their, on their, get the pea grade, pea grades, you'd see, but I don't think there was so much on the, so much on the wheat, of course, there's always some guy that probably, think maybe he's, he should have weighed a little more than, or something like that, but that's common. And then these farmers, these farmers, more than that, the farmers are more, try to, try to, try to cheat on the weights and everything, and I like, I like the, if you, if we weigh, if we'll drive in on the scales, you know,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: maybe, maybe, maybe he'd get a, maybe he'd get a, he'd get out of his truck when he weighed, he weighed him in, see. Then when he got the weight, and vice versa, he'd get out and sometimes he's, when he started, started weighing this, you know, when he started weighing this, he'd get off on, off the scales on this side, he'd get off on this side, the driver's side there, and the way, if you didn't watch him, you'd be standing on the scales. Oh, tell you this. Get kind of catty, some of them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then you know that it's a, the warehouse, the warehouse is supposed to, it's a kind of unwritten law, that the warehouse gets a break on the scales.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, lots of times those farmers, you know, they, they watch all that, and you got to just run up out there and just, there's a guy who says you got to milk it to death. No,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: this old, this old Harry Simpson fellow that I started to work with, you know, I took his instruction away, the way he'd weigh instructions, out of the way, and he showed me how he could, he could run that weight out there just as soon as that beam come down while he, he flipped the lever down along the, he held it right there, see. Well, if you just let it along and just ease it out there, you know, you'd probably get probably 15 or 20 more pounds on there. Ten, maybe. A few more pounds anyway, but that was a, that's an unwritten law, that's the warehouseman's margin.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, but these, these, most of our spotty farmers got onto that door, and so they, they didn't go for that, you had to, you had to ring that key down, just work that key right out there until that, until that break. Well, the first time it was down, you got, yeah, there was a lot of them included in it. It don't matter what you get, of course, they figure the warehouse is getting the best of it. Well, of course, you got to figure this way, there's, there's money in it, or, or there wouldn't, there wouldn't be no warehouses. Now you take a, now you take a sack of wheat,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: now when I first started my house, I mean a lot of it was sacks, you know, you take a, put a sack of wheat in the center of the house all winter, that, that sack of wheat will gain weight. These will do the same thing. If you, if you own me, three thousand bushels of wheat, and you sold me three thousand bushels of wheat in early spring, you sold me, come in there and sold me three thousand bushels of wheat. Well, I'd go to the warehouse and, the way out your three thousand bushels of wheat, there'd be a lot of wheat, there'd be, there'd be quite a little bit of wheat left there. And, they call that the overrun, the same with the peas. That's why these pea men, they figured they make their, they buy big lots of peas and they figured they'll make it on the overrun, see. So that's one, that's one, that's why the warehouse made, that's why the warehouse made their money. It was on the overrun. Well, the sack of wheat will gain weight when they set their old, too long, it would get so much more, so it would gain weight, so will peas. I'm not so sure about barley and oats, I guess they do too, but I know wheat and peas do.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I remember, I found that out before I went to the warehouse business, this place down here where I sit, I had my office in this warehouse, that was originally built as a farmer's union, that was a farmer's outfit. The farmers built that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you know about that farmer's union? What was that all about?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, the way it started here in Moscow, a bunch of farmers went together and they built their own elevator.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they market their grain through this elevator, see, and they got to hire their manager, just kind of like the co -op. It's the same thing, this just didn't get too full, but anyway, they built that elevator down there. And then right across the street from where the lumberyard is, they had a store, a farmer's union store. And this fellow and I said, the boss was always holding his wheat and the bank wound up with the place. And he had, I don't know, he had about two crops of wheat in there. And then he sold it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And when he sold his wheat, well this fellow that was running, we was down there one day and the manager says to him, Billy, what are you going to do with the rest of your wheat?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And this fellow, the manager says, well, he says, I sold it all.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Several pounds of wheat over there in the warehouse, several, quite a bit left over there in the warehouse after we weighed out your tickets. So we went over there and right out here, he had quite a little pile of wheat over there left, because it was overrun on wheat. If it wasn't in the penthouse, they would never have said nothing to him about it, but this was farmer's owned. This fellow told him it was his wheat, so he turned around and sold his over on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened with the farmer's union?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they finally went on, they couldn't make it. I wonder why they couldn't. I don't know, anyway, this, let me see, this, I guess this,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: when this co -op started, the one fellow I worked for, now, I don't remember, I think they bought their building. And another big, grand man in Moscow was Mark P. Miller. Now, this co -op that I worked for, they bought him out. He owned lots of warehouses and he owned a flour mill. But the flour mill had closed down and they built an elevator over there and then finally the grain growers got too big because they bought him out. Now, the manager of the grain growers today is, he was the manager of the Mark P. Miller outfit when I started to work for the grain growers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Uh -huh. And then this manager that I worked for, the grain growers, he retired and when they bought Mark P. Miller out, they took this fellow, took their manager in as assistant manager, see, and he worked, he's the younger man than my boss was, and he worked until he retired, until my boss retired, and then they put him in this blue box. He's the manager now, the manager of the whole works.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, we talked a little about, before about, you know, sometimes you might have trouble with some men, you know, you had to, as the chief, to make,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: you know, a lot of people laugh, but I was wondering, did you have any other, was there anything that was especially challenging or that would cause problems besides sometimes the men in the work that you had to do?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: well, you know that, you know that I don't care what the head man is of any concern, he's gonna have, he's gonna have problems with somebody in some way or form. If you're the boss, why, the fellow that you got hired, you're gonna hire a man that he knows more about your business than you do. You know, that's a set effect. Well, that's the same way we were in my business there. Well, the fellow that they knew more and more about it than I did, they figured they should do it the way that, the way they wanted to do it. And I found it and I had lots of men in fifteen years while I was there and I find lots of fellows go out looking for a man to run an elevator. Where else they wanted, they'd like to have a man, they wanted to like to have an experienced man. Well, I'm just the other way. If you never, if you didn't know nothing about elevator, why you were the man, you were the man that I'm looking for. Because when I put you in that elevator and I explained to you how to do this, how to do it, how to run it, how to do it, well you would do it that way. But if you'd had experience, well you'd know a way better than I did and you'd try to run it your way and first thing you know you're all fouled up. You get too smart. I never did hire a man that was too dumb to run one but I had him that was too smart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the kind of thing where somebody who would do it his own way and would do it wrong, what would be the sort of thing where you had to know the right way to do it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: you know you have to be careful to keep from, one particular thing is you have to watch out and ask them not to get these things mixed. You got wheat, probably two or three different kinds of wheat that you don't mix. Of course not in the late years you did. When I first started they did, they used to have a red wheat and what they call white wheat. But now of course they don't have very little red wheat anymore. And then they had, you see you had a brewing barley and then you had a feed barley and they got to keep it but of course all oats, that's all the same, oats are oats. But there's wheat and the bar is, there's different kinds that you had to keep separate.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So, you know what, I think that's the job, I didn't have too much trouble, I had trouble some.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I still like to get around warehouses and harvest time, as long as I've been away from it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It's funny you have...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Warehousing, alright, the worst thing about warehousing is if you have a wet season.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And you know,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: grain, you're not supposed to take in any grain under a certain percentage of moisture, not over 12%, moisture you see. If you've got a wet season, you know, just during the sunshine, the farmer he'd get out and soon as he'd knock it out of the head, well here he comes in and he's a load of wheat and then you won't take it and then you've got a fight on your hands. Well that was my biggest trouble for a long time, I had my orders and I knew it in the books, don't take it under 12 % from them. And at the warehouse, every warehouse we had a tester, see. You test a man's grain and you tell him, no we can't take that, it's too wet. And he'd probably jump all over you and then he'd take your sweet and come running into the main office.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he'd go running into town to the main office and see what I got, I'll show you whether you take it or not.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But when I told him he couldn't take it, he didn't get nowhere but taking it to the main office. Did it cause a real fire danger if you took wheat that was... Yeah, real heat. Yes, real heat.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But they don't care as long as they can get off their truck, well that's all. I just fell out that door, I guess about this old friend of mine too, he'd come down after a load of wheat and I told him, Miller I can't take that, that's just too wet. And he just about threw up the ramp on the sidewalk.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I said, he said, well why don't you tell him, he says you're not going to stop me from cutting. He said I'm going to go home and go to cutting and put mine in the grain rail. I said, well you're not, I said I'm going to tell you something, you're not that big of a fool to put that wet wheat in your own grain rail. And then instead of that he turned around and he buzzed into town to see the boss. But he didn't cut and put in the rain either. Well he probably figured that being your friend you'd let him get away with it. Yeah, he probably did, he got off of mad though when I wouldn't take his grain. He thought that was an awful dirty trick for me not taking his grain just because we were friends I should take his whether there's water running out of it or not. And then he's going to go back and get on his combine and go to putting it in his own grain rail. And I said, you're not that big of a fool to put that wet wheat in your grain rail. And then he come to town and the boss told him, well if he ain't too wet it must be too wet. He said, he's got the same tester that I've got. It seems like you learned a business pretty darn fast. Yes I did, I did. Well you see I was raised on the farm. And then to go and work in different grains I got a lot of responsibility buying seed wheat and stuff like that. Grading grains and one thing or another. I got to go out real good. The only thing I'd be weak in would be the selling of course. The selling isn't too much because you see if you come in and, if you came in there and the price was a dollar and so much a bushel, you decide to sell after coming in that morning, that day of sell, okay. I'll give you a check for it and then before the market closed, well I'd sell it again. See I'd turn loose and I wouldn't keep, I wouldn't carry it over to the next day. I sell it, I buy it today, I sell it today. Well they don't gamble on it. Farmers gamble on it but they don't. If you come in and sell me your wheat today, I'm going to sell it today. Whatever that price is, I sell it to you today, well then I sell it and get my margin on the other end. I don't keep it until the next day, maybe it'll be better and the officer, I get rid of it that day. I don't fool with it. If I buy 100 ,000 bushels today, I sell 100 ,000.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because that way you always keep your shirt clean. But if you hold it, buy 100 ,000 today, I say well I might make a few cents if I sell it tomorrow and then she might drop. Next day you come out and make a drop, well then you got eggs all over your face. Come in!

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: A corn wall and your son and somebody brought him out to the house and dynamited the house he's living in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the deal on that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well I'll tell you, I don't remember exactly what the deal was at the time but it seemed like he ran a store, a corn wall. And he had two dollars and a son. And he was pretty good, he wasn't an old man but I guess he was in his 50s maybe, maybe he wasn't that old. Anyway, they got to having a lot of family trouble anyway. And so the old man, he moved in another house right across the road in front of his store, they lived up overhead in the store. And he moved in a little house across the road and then he just moved out and that's why they said his son did it, it blowed him up. Well they put a crooked dynamite in there and blew a hole in the roof and they said they blew him out, out the top of it but I don't know what he did or not. But anyway there's an explosion in there and the old man's in there and he came out alive. But they was having some kind of family trouble, I don't know what it was in there in their old age. It seemed like there was kids and the old lady was against the old man.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Did you ever hear the story about when the old Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Watkins got shot?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yes I remember hearing about this. I guess that was about the time we came to the country, about that time I don't know. Around 1900 or something like that. Yeah, 19 -1. Somewhere around that, we hadn't been here long, I remember. I've heard it lots of times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have you heard people say about what Will Steffen was doing? What have you heard people say about Will Steffen and why?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't want to say for sure whether it was him or not, but we just thought it was him. But he was pretty well, there was lots of talk about him. When I was, oh we were back in the olden days about 1904 fire along the way when I was a little kid. I know there was a fellow camped over in the timber over in the school section or whatever it was. We lived in the tent up there and so on and the kids were afraid to go up in there. Because the kids said it was a wild day. Whether it was or not I don't know, but I don't think so.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You want to tell me when I get to the festival?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well you know, I don't see your good people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When we were talking about prejudice before, you said, when we were talking about prejudice, you said that the progress of the people, I think you said it has to have blood shed for there to be progress. You know, to move ahead. I was thinking about that when I was looking through some of the things we discussed already. I was wondering, that's all you said about it at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you mean by that, that you have to have blood shed to have progress for a people?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I didn't get to get your question Sam. You said I was talking about prejudice. The advances of a people require blood shed. It takes blood shed for their... Because we were talking about civil rights in the south. And you were saying blood shed is something that you have to... Well I think I saw a very good remark that there's very few good things that came to people without blood shed. Were there without blood shed. Well, so just take the... getting things straightened out. Well just like this country was, we had to have a civil war. And you take the same thing that's going on in Angola now. There's a civil war over there. They're trying to get their problems straightened out. It's going to be blood shed before they ever do it. It is already. So, and you take it to your back to Bible days. It's usually blood shed before that people can settle their difficulties. For some reason or other, I don't know why it has to be, but that's usually the way it is. And it don't seem like how highly civilized we get. We still go to war and settle our difficulties. And she had blood shed before we get a difficulty, so... I had the idea when we were talking before too that maybe... Well we talked about World War I and you said something like, maybe we didn't gain anything by that war, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And is that the way you feel?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That really the war didn't... No, really. I don't think that was supposed to be the war in all wars, but I don't think it was nothing gained.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And just like this conflict we just got out of. We look at the 50 ,000 lives over there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And that, for what?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In other words, the way I felt about that war over there, we were intervening and entering into a civil war anyway. And I figured we were kind of mental in somebody else's business, just the same way as this Andy Gordon situation in Africa now.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I figured that Cuba, Russia, and those people, I figured they were butting in on somebody else's affair, isn't it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And after it's all over with, why, they'll settle it among themselves anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in World War I, Gene, did you believe that that was going to be the last big war?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I did. I did at that time. Yes, I did. I figured I would. I sure wasn't looking for another war, but I said when I was over there at this German prisoners camp, you talk to some of those German prisoners, they were very bitter against France. They wasn't against the United States, but they always said, I had more than one of them tell me that France was going to have to pay for this. But I didn't think there'd be another war. About 20 years from then, I thought maybe I didn't think I'd ever live to see another war.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When you were a kid, and we talked about prejudice before, and you were saying that there really wasn't very much, not very much when you were a kid. I was wondering, was there any when you were a kid that you remember of times when you would feel, when you knew that you were being...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Mistreated on account of my color?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, not too free. Sam, I can't say that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I can't say that I've done anything like that, of course. I tell you, if I had been raised in the city, I figured I would have had more of a chance for that, but I was raised out in the whole of the States, and I don't know whether you did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And another thing, there was four of us boys,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and we didn't have to run around to go other places to have fun or do things. We did things at our own pace. We entertained ourselves to a lot. And so therefore, we weren't thrown in with so much communication with the outside world, as I say, because it wasn't any of that until after we'd come over here and started the school here in town. That's why I was out there in the country. Of course, we always had... well, you might have to come here now.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Our place was kind of a gathering place on weekends, because we always had so much going on up there. And there we had horses to fool with and different projects to work with, and kids in horseshoe and one thing or another. Kids always walked to our place, and if there were two friends, they wouldn't show up naturally. Before I just actually being hurt by prejudice, I think I told you what the worst that I ever... first, and the worst that I ever ran into prejudice was when I started to enlist in the Army.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That was my worst for being called prejudice, but just because others didn't. I think, of course, I've had kids to get mad at me and to call me nigger, such as that, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid and that kind of thing happened, what would you do if a kid called you that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you get your own man?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I ignored it, because I figured it this way, so I could get up in the easy way.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It wasn't...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The kid learned to call his parents, probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it stop you from wanting to be friends with a kid like that? Huh? Would it stop you from wanting to be his friend?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well... Well, I'm glad you know how kids are.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They never...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, the next minute while he's up there wanting me to do something for him or do something that way, I didn't think it would. Yes.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, I mean, just to come out and... And this bar of milk or anything like that is for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You thought that was a thing to do?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's a different story, but... But you could see... I probably saw them worse things than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did you feel, when you were a kid, did you feel very much aware of the difference in color?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I didn't. No, it's just a funny thing, you know that...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When I came, I left the South, came out here, I was just a little kid, and to tell you the truth about it, I can remember about the South a lot. My dad, he had a farm, he had cotton, he had a big hog pasture, a hog lot we called it, and he hauled a... and he had a contractor, he hauled a slop from the hotel from this town, see, and he always had two or three black boys to do that for him. And he always had... and I remember when they picked cotton, they always had a bunch of black people, women and men, picking cotton for a few days, whatever it takes to get what little you had to pick. And outside of that, the people that I know, the zon -on's that he showcased with, they were all white, there was more of them, I'd see more of them than I would be able to pick, of course. He was always... he would run a big lot down there, took him towards the pasture, towards the people that he dealt with, mostly white, and I came out here and then... Now, truthfully stated, I never saw any black people until I went in the army. I didn't know there was that many in the world. No, I didn't, you know. When I got high school, I went Spokane a time or two, and went to church up there, not a bad few, but up until I was 21 years old, it's the very few black people that I'd ever had any contact with whatsoever.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, when you were a kid, I mean, you thought of yourself as being black at the same time. Oh yeah, sure, I knew that I was black, I knew that, that's about what I knew. But I had my youngest brother, I don't believe he ever realized that he was a black man, I don't believe he was a kid that way, just something that didn't seem like it was any different to him.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, of course we heard my parents talk about the South and the difference between races and the trouble, and all the troubles in the tribe, and the prejudices and all of that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I got told that before. We were breaking horses, or we were doing something. So, that's the way I grew up, so I didn't run into the sculler barrier too much. And I figured it was pleasant, alright.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah. Well, they took him in, of course, to be honest, I don't know why I heard that they went to dances up there, and of course, that's one thing about us kids, there's only two kids, out of five kids there's only two of them that ever did dance, that was my sister and my next wife and my youngest brother. But now, of course, down here we had parties, parties go from one neighborhood to another in the wintertime, and have parties and things like that, of course we always liked, but we never danced. And later days, school functions, and different neighborhoods and all that, we was a chef.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get joked or teased about it? Did kids ever tease you about it or joke with you about it, about the color difference?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because I still sometimes hear jokes about it. I've been in times, guys that always tell some nigger jokes, I always figured they were just telling it for my benefit, but maybe it was and maybe it wasn't. But anyway, what I was concerned about, it wasn't one ear and not the other, because I just figured, well, you're just exposing your own ignorance, not mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any real close friends when you were a kid or any special friends? When you were a kid or growing up, did you have any special pals that you palled around outside the family?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, I had friends, special friends, but I wasn't so much of that as some of my younger brothers, they usually was more, probably with a bunch of kids, and I was more of a loner myself, but I had my brothers. They always had a bunch of kids at our place and come to stay all night and they'd go to other places and stay all night, well I did it too, and the kids did, such as that. But just like some people do, get all tied up in one certain pal, and you can't turn around without him, I never was that way. I think it's been more that way since I've been married, since I was already married life, the fellows that I met, the two of us, that is their families, them and his wife and my wife, we'd paddle together that way. Every weekend we'd probably go way off in the mountains on a fishing trip or something like that, and after we had a little girl, and they had two little kids, more pally that way since we was married than when I was a kid, but I had brothers that, they had friends just like that, you couldn't see one without the other, but I never was that way, and I stayed until after we was married, then when I was a kid, my family was that way, we went to the other, but I never was.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I never was the guy that put all my eggs in one basket.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think about it, you're growing up, I can really understand what you're saying, why race prejudice wouldn't be a problem. I imagine that probably it meant a lot more to your parents in a way than it did to the kids, because I imagine your parents had seen a lot more. Oh, I know, well I know they have, they saw all this, they knew all about it, so they had experienced it, they knew what it was before they ever came out here, they knew what it was all about, sure they did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: at least like us kids, the last we got off here,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think that's what I was thinking, I can't remember anything in real time that even my dad, I know my mother did, but my mother never, she didn't go, she was kind of home, she didn't go too much,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: but my dad, I remember him talking about anytime where he's got a slap in the face because of all the color of his color, but I don't think so, that's nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he felt about this country around here, as it went as far as prejudice, I mean whether it was really a good place and how he felt about it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, You said to me something once about, we talked a little bit that westerners could be more prejudiced than southerners, that that was Yeah, that's what I got that from my father now, he, Now you take, you know some of his closest friends, when he came out here, some of his closest associates were people from the south. Yes, now his actual, you know he had hound, he'd do a lot of hunting, and so one of the guys that hunt with him, so many of them were southerners,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I can see,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Why is it,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: why do people make people stand off, stand off on a black person, you know, because that, they never get, Well, from my own experience, because I grew up, I didn't see no nigger until I was 21, well, what about the, the white kids that screwed up and never, never saw one, and actually they're gonna,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Probably, I'd be trying to stand over some wonder and see, that's just the way I felt when I started high school here in Moscow. I was the only black that came in when I started school, well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Some of the kids, some of the kids, they were standoffish, you know, they didn't bother me or didn't say nothing, but, They just kind of gave me plenty of room, you know, I was a little bit, wasn't very long, Well, they found out I stood upright and walked just to talk, just the same as they did, the only difference in me and them was the color, but after they found out I wasn't, well, I wasn't an animal, a beast, well then they began to kind of, Well, of course, what was wrong with why I was on the track team, I was on the football team and everything else I wanted to get on to, and this would help me do more than I wanted to, so, I could see their side of it, because, you see, they were kind of, kind of amazed, to start with. Have you ever discussed this with Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Settle, about what her experience was like when she was a kid growing up?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, hers is pretty much like mine, she grew up on a farm down here, down here out of Pomeroy there, and her dad, and she had two brothers, of course, they were a little more,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think they were a little more to themselves, oh, than the girls were, than we were.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course, they were aware of the prejudices there, same as we were, we knew it was there, but we just didn't, it just didn't get in our road. I think if we'd have been in town, or some place like that, where we were depending on,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: depending on the town, it would be different, do you think your father felt, do you know if he felt that he was held back by it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't know, I never heard him say anything about that, whether he thought the hipster was hurting him, or anything like that, anyway, he seemed to get what he wanted, and if he always needed help, he always had somebody to do it. I remember, I remember, I don't know, I remember the first, he bought 80 acres of school land up there, up here to see what it was, and he had to pay so much down on it, and when, after he got it, he didn't have the money, he didn't have enough money to pay down on it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I know that,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: it wasn't long, they found out he was short, these different ones were coming off, coming off of him enough money to, so he didn't have enough money to pay down on it, I do remember that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So he could make a down payment on it, but I never, I don't remember, I never heard my dad say anything about being any harder, or running up against a stone wall because he was a black man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the farm, did your family have a lot to do with their neighbors? Did you do much with the contact work?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Sure, sure, we had neighbors, we had friends, we had lots of friends, we were neighbors, we were neighbors with all of us friends. Yes sir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the kind of stuff that neighbors, you know, that you did, you would do with the neighbors? What kind of contacts would you have with them? Would it be over exchanging work and that kind of thing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
