words: Well, there's lots of times, you know, if a neighbor needed help, we'd go work, help them out, and if we needed vice versa, there's lots of times we'd visit back and forth in the wintertime, and that's about the time farmers got time to visit in the wintertime, and we'd always take the women, lots of times, afternoons, the neighbors would come to visit my mother and vice versa, we were neighbors, and we'd go to the farm, and we'd go to the farm with the family, and there would be lots more, but my mother, she didn't go very much herself, she was quite a quiet lady, but she always appreciated people coming to her house, and she went some too, for just a tournament, she didn't go as much as she had coming, there was always somebody at her house, and we had lots of friends, lots of neighbors, and in those days, when I was a kid, you knew your neighbor now, nowadays you don't know who your neighbor is, because they don't socialize like they used to when,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: because when I was a kid, they used to have, in the wintertime, they used to have parties, they were down in different houses all winter long, and now since they got to automobiles, everybody, kids all leave home, they wouldn't stay out at home, they got to go 25, 30 miles off somewhere else, but those days, we had to go horseback, and we had a party right there in the neighborhood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of neighborhood parties were there, were they dancing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, sometimes they'd dance, square dance, some would play pinocle, play cards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did there be music, did somebody play violin?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, somebody could play, some old farmer could play turkey in the school or something like that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we had a lot of horns just the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it would usually be just one instrument or something like that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, maybe somebody could chord on the organ, a piano or something like that, somebody could violin, that's another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be food too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Sometimes there'd be two different, I think in Iowa there's about two different fellows that could play violin and some, and I don't know, somebody, one woman, she chord, she played piano a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be eats at a get together like that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yes, oh yes, oh yes, I wish I had lunch of some kind. Yeah, we used to have some good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's one more thing I want to ask you before I go, because I got to go, but that is, I was going to ask you about your father, what kind of a man do you remember him as being? My father? Yeah, you told me different things that he did, but what kind of a person do you remember him?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'll tell you this, a fellow by the name of Gus Gamble, you probably know the name of the old pioneers. He and I were, he and I was standing down downtown there talking, and then my dad was coming up the other side of the street and he started to cross over to where I was, and Gus Gamble says, here comes old John Settle, the man with a smile for everybody. I thought he just about described my dad pretty much. He was a real sociable guy, a good guy, and he always used to do the right thing, never took advantage of anybody, just a good old man, that's what my dad was. And I think in your travel you don't find many people to say the same thing, I guess John, my dad, he was a black man, that's all. But he was a good, honest, little man, John Settle was.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He had lots of friends.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I guess in the Wells family they were old pioneers of this country, but they were, there's a lot of things that they didn't have in common. Now you take the Wells family, I don't know whether it was their environment or whether they were raised up there in the woods, but they were more, they liked the moonshine, whiskey, and that kind of stuff. Well, that didn't go with my people at all. But the Wells they did, they were all good timers, and we had to have the fun, like the rest of the Woodrats up there. Of course there's a different community, there's those Swedes, and great whiskey, and the food was moonshine.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Your family really wasn't a bit like that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When I was a little girl, I was a little girl, I was a little girl, and I was a little girl. I just liked this drinking and hoo -rah. Of course they called it having fun, but I couldn't see no fun in that. Of course they were just keeping up with the Joneses up there in the woods. Well what about Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wells?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She didn't... She was more of a quiet woman, more like my mother, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like your own mother? She didn't like what her family was doing, but what could she do about it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I guess I was a little bit more quiet. But there was good people, there were good people in their way. And their way is like the way of the 90 % of them up there, was doing the same thing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Did she take a lot of responsibility, Mrs. Wells, around the... No, I tell you, I worked with her up there for about six weeks, I believe, one winter, the winter of 1919. And about all that woman did is to walk down to the nearest store there and keep grub on that table and wash their clothes. And say it very low. It was there, but she and I would get to talk together. Last time I didn't have to suffer, I'd go out there and buy dishes for us up and we'd talk. So that's when I found out that she didn't dislike the way her family lived, about the way they used to whisked and moonshined and all that. She didn't like that. She didn't think too much of that, but nothing she could do about it. And she didn't try to do nothing about it. She just did her work.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she take a life in the field?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yes, we got a long life. And she was just like a slave to Joe. He'd go in there, hollering at her. And got so far from the little old woman. I guess she was used to it. But anyway, she never hollered back at him. He just went on with her work. It was actually just like she was a slave girl from the outfit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did her sons treat her? How did the kids treat her?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, that's what they were. They were all just alike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just like Joe?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, they all were. They can beat old Joe. Of course, he was an old man. But the kids there, they were worse than old men. Of course, they always had whiskey around the house. Old Joe would drink this out. But he didn't go out and play his cane like the boys did. He did most of his very well here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I remember my dad saying one time that he came to town. I think that's about the first time that he knew that this Sugar Pam was up there in Derry. But I don't know whether he had met it or he'd heard of it. I think the first time my dad... I want to say for sure whether it was the first time or not. But it seemed like the first time my dad came to town one day and he met an old Joe and he was drunk then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he say anything about it, your dad?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, he was telling us about it when they came home that this man was drunk.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But you knew that the boys could even beat up on their father. No, no, I never heard of him fighting among them. I never did hear him. Nothing like that at all. I didn't know. He wasn't fighting at all. But I know that the old man, he was very abusive to his wife. I know that. He was very abusive to her. But she just took it. Never said I guess she got used to it. She just took it. Never got back with him, of course.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Took back.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But she sure didn't have... She sure kept the meal on the table, the grub on the table all the time for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Mary there when you were there? Huh? Was Mary living there when you came out here? Did you get to know her at all?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I knew her.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She had...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Mary was up there. She had two kids. She had two little kids from when I was up there, one. I think she had both of them because she married a soldier from Fort Wright up here in Spokane.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he went to...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he left...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He went to World War I, same as I did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he got his first... He went as far as New York. And then they lost track of him. But he went with the company to New York. And it seemed like nobody could never find old Craig Heineher, that man after he got to New York.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He never came back here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, you know, he never... They never came back because they never did hear from him. I know that after I came back from the Army, and after I'd been up there in the woods there, talking to... Working with the Welters, and I heard her side of the story, and Riswell side of the story. And I belonged to the Veterans of Foreign Wars here in Moscow. And I took it up to the Veterans of Foreign Wars to try to locate this man. And we traced him to New York, like they said, and they couldn't find no hide and the error with his zero number nothing after New York all over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's happened to him now?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's a 50.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He never did show up back here? Do you remember how Mary felt about it when you were out there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: How she... Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she think he was coming back or what?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it seemed like...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It seemed to me like that she was kind of reconciled over the deal. That has been about two years. He had been gone.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because he sat down and talked to me, because I got all the details of the whole thing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I took it up with the Veterans of Foreign Wars. I got all the identification, his zero number, whatever I could from him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a soldier when they got married?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was a soldier up here at Fort Wright. Fort Wright, Fort George Wright up here at Spokane. He was a regular soldier.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she know where he was from in the first place?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know. I guess she did know where he was from. He was a smart guy anyway. I guess he spoke two or three different languages.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He said.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But he was... I think he was kind of like the rest of them. He was a drinker. He was a loser. He liked his whiskey.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But he disappeared. He went into the Army again. He went into the Army again when the war broke out. And they traced him in his company until he got to New York.
